html:
Magnus Ladulås, whose real name was Magnus Birgersson, was born around 1240 and died on December 18, 1290, on the island of Visingsö. He reigned as King of Sweden from 1275 to 1290. Magnus was the son of Birger Jarl and Ingeborg Eriksdotter. He was married to Helvig of Holstein and was the father of, among others, his successor, Birger Magnusson.

== Childhood and Years as Duke == Very little is known about Magnus's childhood and upbringing. The names of two of his teachers are known: Björn Näf, a member of the Royal Council, and a Magister Palne, who seems to have been involved in the education of all of Birger Jarl's sons. It is also known that Magnus was "somewhat dark-skinned and thin," which led to a lesser-known nickname: "ketlaböter" (meaning something like "kettle boy"), given to him by his sister-in-law, Sofia.

In 1255, Birger Jarl obtained the Pope's blessing for his plan to allocate "certain shares in the kingdom" to his sons. From this time onwards, Magnus began using his father's old seal with a new inscription: "iunior dux sweorum" (younger duke of Swedes). Upon Birger Jarl's death in 1266, Magnus inherited his father's former title of Earl, but as a duchy, lacking the same powers that his father had held. As Duke, Magnus used Birger Jarl's former title: "Dei gratia dux sweorum" ("By the grace of God, Duke of Swedes"). During the years 1267–1273, Swedish sources do not indicate any evidence that Magnus' position undermined his brother Valdemar's authority as an absolute king. However, according to the saga of Valdemar's brother-in-law, Magnus Ladulås, significant political events occurred. Magnus Ladulås and Valdemar were married to daughters of the Danish King Eric Plogpenning, so the Norwegian king had good reason to carefully monitor political developments in Sweden.

The most important element in Swedish domestic politics during these years was the third of Birger Jarl's sons, Erik, who was dissatisfied with his marginalized role and therefore began working with Magnus to weaken Valdemar's position. From around 1272, this undermining activity began to yield visible results. As the Eric Chronicle states, the Duke's "influence" became greater than the King's from this time onwards, and Magnus, supported by a group of powerful nobles, began demanding a larger share of power.

In 1274, a crisis arose for King Valdemar, who took countermeasures to prevent Magnus from seizing power. A letter dated August 22, 1274, indicates that Magnus had advanced his position to the point where even his seal appeared on documents that would normally have been sealed only by the king. Papal letters dated January 9, 1275, which Valdemar obtained, revealed that a civil war was imminent. According to these letters, "elements of unrest" within the kingdom had proclaimed Valdemar's brother, Magnus, as an alternative king, a "de facto King of Sweden." Magnus and Erik sought support from Eric Klipping, who, through an agreement in Sønderborg, pledged to provide the brothers with 100 heavily armed knights with their retinue (a total of 500–600 men). In return, Magnus and Erik promised 6,000 silver marks. With this Danish military assistance, Valdemar was defeated at the Battle of Hova on June 14, 1275.

Valdemar fled across the border into Norway but decided to return and was subsequently captured, leading to negotiations. All parties were fully aware that a military victory in a field battle at Tiveden would not be a sustainable basis for permanently deposing Valdemar. Magnus and his advisors knew that they had to persuade Valdemar to renounce the crown. Therefore, at a meeting of nobles in Västergötland following the Battle of Hova, it was decided that the kingdom should be divided, with Valdemar receiving Västergötland, Dalsland, Värmland, and Småland. In response to these areas, Valdemar issued a declaration at Asknäs in which he relinquished his "inherited kingdom" to Duke Magnus. Through a formal ceremony at Mora äng, Valdemar renounced the crown, and Magnus was proclaimed king on July 22, 1275.

== Magnus' Reign ==

=== Domestic Policy === Magnus was formally installed on July 22, 1275, at Uppsala Cathedral, and he appointed his brother Erik as Duke. However, Erik died later the same year, and no new duke was appointed. Instead, Magnus' younger brother Bengt became a close advisor from this point onward. The bishops of Sweden accepted Magnus as the new king, and the coronation took place on Pentecost Sunday, May 24, 1276, at Uppsala Cathedral.

At a meeting in Skänninge, likely during the winter of 1277–1278, Valdemar was forced to reiterate his abdication after a failed attempt in the autumn of 1277 to recruit the Margraves of Brandenburg for his cause. After Valdemar agreed to be styled "quondam rex" (former king), he was restored to possession of his hereditary lands and granted rights to the crown's holdings in Västergötland and Östergötland as a conditional fiefdom.

Magnus' reign has been characterized as "the period of the definitive establishment of the privileged society." Magnus, during his coronation in 1276, promised a general charter granting the church exemption from all state taxes, and this promise was fulfilled at Queen Helvig's coronation in Söderköping on July 29, 1281. At an assembly of nobles held at A...

How certain are we about this?

For people living in the Middle Ages, there are often contemporary written sources (letters, chronicles, church documents), so the main points are usually well-documented. However, these sources were mostly written by the Church and those in power, and they are unevenly preserved, which colors and limits our understanding.
tldr: The text describes Magnus Ladulås, who became King of Sweden in 1275 after a power struggle with his brother, Valdemar.
lattlast:
Magnus Ladulås was the son of Birger Jarl and lived in the 13th century. He began his career as a duke, a title for someone who governs a region, but he desired more power than his brother, King Valdemar, possessed. After several years of conflict and threats of war, Magnus emerged victorious and was appointed king in July 1275.

When Magnus became king, many things changed in Sweden. He promised to grant the church greater freedom from state regulations, which was a significant part of his policy. His reign is often described as the period when Swedish society established its various privileges for different groups.

We know much about these events because there are old documents and writings from the church and other powerful figures. Although these sources are ancient and not always entirely complete, they provide a clear picture of how the power struggles unfolded and how Magnus ruled the country.
